title: Microsoft's Encryption Software Squeeze
subtitle: Locking out VeraCrypt developers reveals how platform gatekeepers can kill security tools they don't control.

sources.angle: Developer says Microsoft account lock threatens Windows users with boot-up issues
Tech community outraged over Microsoft's ability to control independent security software distribution
Broader context of platform control over software distribution and user choice

brief.bottomLine: Microsoft's lockout of the VeraCrypt encryption developer exposes how platform gatekeepers can effectively ban security tools that compete with their own products — a power that makes independent security impossible.

hiddenBets.assumption: Microsoft locked the account due to routine policy violations rather than competitive concerns
hiddenBets.whyItMightBeWrong: The timing and target suggest this could be about controlling encryption software that bypasses Microsoft's own security systems

hiddenBets.assumption: Users can always find alternative distribution methods for banned security software
hiddenBets.whyItMightBeWrong: Platform integration becomes so deep that alternative distribution breaks core functionality

brief.realDisagreement: Whether platform gatekeepers should be able to restrict security software distribution. Microsoft defenders say platforms need control to prevent malware and maintain system stability. Security advocates say this power enables censorship of tools that protect against those same platforms. Both positions are reasonable, but they can't coexist. I lean toward the security advocate position — platform control over security tools creates inherent conflicts of interest that threaten user protection. But what we'd give up is the convenience and integration that platform control provides.
brief.whatNoOneIsSaying: Microsoft probably sees independent encryption tools as a competitive threat to their cloud services business model, which depends on accessing user data.

whoPays.how: Loss of access to independent encryption tools that don't share data with platform providers
whoPays.when: Immediately as alternative tools become harder to install and maintain

whoPays.how: Distribution channels controlled by the platforms they're trying to protect against
whoPays.when: As platform policies become more restrictive over the next year

whoPays.how: Reduced access to encryption tools that governments can't backdoor or monitor
whoPays.when: As governments pressure platforms to restrict security software

scenarios.name: Security Software Exodus
scenarios.whatHappens: Independent security developers move to alternative platforms or distribution methods
scenarios.signal: Security software companies announce they're leaving Microsoft's ecosystem

scenarios.name: Regulatory Intervention
scenarios.whatHappens: Governments force platforms to allow security software distribution without restrictions
scenarios.signal: Antitrust authorities open investigations into platform control over security tools

scenarios.name: Platform Security Monopoly
scenarios.whatHappens: Microsoft and other platforms successfully control the security software market through distribution control
scenarios.signal: Independent security tools become increasingly difficult to install or maintain

brief.whatWouldChange: Evidence that Microsoft's restrictions genuinely prevent security threats would justify platform control. Evidence that restricted tools are being banned for competitive rather than security reasons would prove the censorship concern.
brief.whatHappened: Microsoft locked out the lead developer of VeraCrypt, the most widely used open-source disk encryption software, from their developer account in April 2026. The lockout prevents VeraCrypt from receiving code-signing certificates needed to distribute software on Windows without triggering security warnings, effectively making the tool unusable for most Windows users.
